text: Landlord may retain all or part of the security deposit for cleaning, repainting, carpet wear, general wear and tear, administrative costs, or any other condition landlord deems necessary.
label: red-flag
why: This lets the landlord charge the tenant for ordinary wear and vague administrative costs.
market_standard: Security deposit deductions should be limited to unpaid rent, documented damages beyond ordinary wear, and itemized lawful charges.
counter: Security deposit deductions must be limited to unpaid rent and itemized damage beyond ordinary wear and tear, with receipts provided within the legally required return window.
demo_hook: The landlord can keep your deposit for normal wear.

text: Security deposit will be returned after landlord completes inspection and accounting.
label: unusual
why: There is no firm deadline, which can make it hard for the tenant to recover the deposit quickly.
market_standard: The lease should identify the statutory return deadline or a specific number of days.
counter: Security deposit must be returned with an itemized statement within the deadline required by applicable law.

text: If rent is late, tenant will pay a late fee of $150 plus $25 per day until rent is paid in full.
label: red-flag
why: Daily compounding late fees can become punitive and disproportionate.
market_standard: Late fees should have a short grace period and a reasonable cap.
counter: Late fees apply only after a 5-day grace period and are capped at the lesser of $50 or the maximum allowed by law.

text: Tenant is responsible for all repairs, replacements, maintenance, pest control, appliance failures, plumbing issues, electrical issues, and habitability-related expenses during the lease term.
label: red-flag
why: This shifts core landlord maintenance duties to the tenant.
market_standard: Tenants handle minor damage they cause; landlords handle habitability, systems, appliances provided with the unit, and structural repairs.
counter: Tenant is responsible only for damage caused by tenant or guests. Landlord remains responsible for habitability, building systems, included appliances, and structural repairs.
demo_hook: Tenant pays for plumbing and electrical failures.

text: Landlord may enter the premises at any time for inspection, repairs, showings, maintenance, emergencies, or any business purpose.
label: red-flag
why: This removes meaningful notice and privacy protections.
market_standard: Entry should require reasonable written notice except for emergencies.
counter: Landlord may enter only with at least 24 hours' notice during reasonable hours, except in emergencies or as otherwise required by law.
demo_hook: Landlord can enter at any time.

text: If tenant terminates early, tenant remains liable for all rent through the end of the lease term, regardless of whether landlord re-rents the premises.
label: red-flag
why: This ignores the landlord's duty to mitigate by trying to re-rent the unit.
market_standard: Tenant may owe reasonable reletting costs and rent until the unit is re-rented, subject to mitigation.
counter: If tenant terminates early, landlord must make commercially reasonable efforts to re-rent, and tenant liability ends when a replacement tenancy begins.
demo_hook: You could pay rent after someone else moves in.

text: Tenant may not assign, sublet, host, license, or otherwise permit any other person to occupy the premises for any period without landlord's sole and absolute discretion.
label: unusual
why: Absolute discretion can block reasonable life changes or roommate replacements.
market_standard: Landlord consent should not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.
counter: Tenant may request assignment or sublet approval, and landlord consent may not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.

text: Landlord may increase rent, fees, utility charges, or other amounts at any time upon written notice.
label: red-flag
why: A fixed-term lease should not allow unilateral price changes during the term.
market_standard: Rent should be fixed during the lease term except for agreed pass-throughs clearly stated in the lease.
counter: Rent and recurring fees are fixed during the lease term unless both parties agree in writing or a specific lawful pass-through is listed here.

text: Tenant agrees to pay all utilities, shared charges, building charges, service fees, billing fees, and any other charges allocated by landlord.
label: red-flag
why: Open-ended fees make the real monthly cost unpredictable.
market_standard: Utility responsibility and allocation formulas should be explicit.
counter: Tenant is responsible only for utilities and fees specifically listed in this lease, with shared utilities allocated by a disclosed formula and supporting bills available on request.

text: Tenant waives the right to bring or participate in any class action and agrees all disputes must be resolved by binding arbitration selected by landlord.
label: red-flag
why: This limits the tenant's ability to join claims with others and gives landlord control over the forum.
market_standard: Dispute clauses should preserve statutory tenant rights and use a neutral forum.
counter: Disputes may be resolved in any court or forum available under applicable law, and tenant does not waive non-waivable statutory rights.

text: Tenant shall indemnify landlord from all claims, losses, damages, costs, and attorney fees arising from the premises, regardless of cause.
label: red-flag
why: The tenant could be liable for claims caused by the landlord or building defects.
market_standard: Tenant indemnity should be limited to tenant's negligence, misconduct, or breach.
counter: Tenant indemnity applies only to claims caused by tenant's negligence, willful misconduct, or breach, and excludes landlord negligence or building defects.

text: This lease automatically renews for another 12 months unless tenant gives written notice at least 120 days before expiration.
label: unusual
why: A long notice window can trap tenants into another full term.
market_standard: Renewal should require affirmative consent or a reasonable 30-60 day notice window.
counter: Renewal requires written agreement by both parties, or tenant may opt out with 30 days' notice before expiration.
demo_hook: Miss a deadline and you owe another year.

text: Tenant must provide 90 days' written move-out notice or pay two additional months of rent after vacating.
label: red-flag
why: The penalty is harsh and may surprise tenants at the end of a fixed term.
market_standard: Move-out notice should be reasonable and any fee should reflect actual damages.
counter: Tenant will provide 30 days' move-out notice before the lease end date, with no extra rent owed after surrender unless required by law.
demo_hook: Leaving on time can still cost two months.

text: Tenant must carry renter's insurance with $1,000,000 liability coverage and name landlord as additional insured.
label: unusual
why: The coverage requirement may be higher than typical renter policies and additional insured language can be overbroad.
market_standard: Reasonable renter's insurance limits are common, but requirements should be achievable and clear.
counter: Tenant will maintain commercially available renter's insurance with reasonable liability limits and may name landlord as an interested party, not additional insured.

text: Tenant shall pay landlord's attorney fees and costs in any dispute, regardless of outcome.
label: red-flag
why: One-way fee clauses discourage tenants from asserting valid rights.
market_standard: Fee provisions should be mutual and tied to the prevailing party.
counter: Attorney fees may be awarded only to the prevailing party where allowed by law, and the provision applies equally to both parties.
demo_hook: You pay their lawyer even if you win.

text: Tenant knowingly waives trial by jury for all claims involving the premises, landlord, property manager, or this lease.
label: unusual
why: This can remove a procedural right tenants may not realize they are giving up.
market_standard: Consumer leases often avoid broad procedural waivers or make them conspicuous and mutual.
counter: Delete the jury trial waiver, or make any procedural waiver mutual, conspicuous, and subject to applicable law.

text: Tenant may not withhold rent, repair and deduct, or offset any amount for landlord's failure to perform repairs.
label: red-flag
why: This may conflict with tenant remedies for habitability problems.
market_standard: The lease should not waive non-waivable statutory repair or habitability remedies.
counter: Nothing in this lease limits tenant's rights or remedies under applicable habitability, repair, or consumer protection laws.

text: Tenant accepts the premises as-is and waives all claims concerning condition, habitability, defects, pests, mold, appliances, and building systems.
label: red-flag
why: Habitability duties often cannot be waived, and as-is language can hide serious problems.
market_standard: The lease may acknowledge current condition but should preserve habitability obligations.
counter: Tenant accepts visible condition at move-in, but landlord remains responsible for habitability, code compliance, and required repairs.

text: Tenant acknowledges the premises may contain mold and releases landlord from all mold-related claims.
label: red-flag
why: Broad mold releases can shift health and remediation risk to the tenant.
market_standard: Known conditions should be disclosed and remediation obligations should remain with the landlord when required.
counter: Landlord must disclose known mold conditions and promptly remediate moisture or mold issues as required by law.

text: Tenant is solely responsible for all pest control, extermination, and related treatment costs regardless of source.
label: unusual
why: Pest issues can be building-wide or pre-existing.
market_standard: Tenant handles pest issues caused by tenant conduct; landlord handles pre-existing or building-wide infestations.
counter: Tenant is responsible only for pest issues caused by tenant or guests. Landlord is responsible for pre-existing, structural, or building-wide pest issues.
demo_hook: Building pest problems become your bill.

text: Tenant may not make alterations, paint, install fixtures, or make structural changes without landlord's prior written consent.
label: standard
why: Landlords commonly control physical changes to the unit.
market_standard: Consent requirements for alterations are common if reasonable and not used to block minor reversible changes.
counter: Tenant may make minor, non-damaging, reversible changes that are removed before move-out, subject to reasonable building rules.

text: No guest may stay overnight without landlord's prior written consent, and any unauthorized guest is a material breach.
label: unusual
why: Overnight guest bans are intrusive and hard to comply with.
market_standard: Leases usually limit long-term occupancy rather than ordinary guests.
counter: Tenant may have ordinary guests. Guests staying more than 14 consecutive days or 30 days in a year require landlord approval, not to be unreasonably withheld.

text: Any change in occupants requires landlord approval and a $500 administrative processing fee.
label: unusual
why: A high administrative fee may deter reasonable roommate changes.
market_standard: Occupancy changes may require screening, but fees should be disclosed, reasonable, and tied to actual costs.
counter: Occupant changes require reasonable approval and any screening or administrative fees must be disclosed, lawful, and limited to actual third-party costs.

text: Any breach by tenant triggers liquidated damages equal to three months' rent in addition to all other remedies.
label: red-flag
why: A fixed penalty for any breach may be excessive and duplicative.
market_standard: Fees should approximate actual damages and avoid stacking penalties.
counter: Delete the liquidated damages clause. Landlord may recover only lawful, documented actual damages and remedies allowed by law.
demo_hook: Any breach costs three months rent.

text: If tenant holds over after expiration, tenant owes double rent for each day of holdover plus all consequential damages.
label: standard
why: Holdover rent premiums are common, though consequential damages should be reasonable and documented.
market_standard: Holdover rent premiums are common when clearly disclosed and lawful.
counter: Holdover rent may not exceed the maximum allowed by law, and consequential damages must be documented and reasonably foreseeable.
demo_hook: Common but should be capped by law.

text: Tenant acknowledges that no oral statements, promises, advertisements, listings, or representations are binding unless written in this lease.
label: standard
why: Integration clauses are common, but important promises should be written into the lease.
market_standard: The written lease should include all important promises about repairs, concessions, parking, appliances, and amenities.
counter: Add any promised repairs, concessions, parking, appliances, or amenities as written lease addenda before signing.

text: Landlord is not liable for theft, fire, flood, water intrusion, utility failure, personal injury, or property damage from any cause.
label: red-flag
why: This tries to disclaim liability even for landlord negligence or building conditions.
market_standard: Landlords may disclaim some personal property risk but not their own negligence or statutory duties.
counter: Landlord's limitation of liability does not apply to landlord negligence, willful misconduct, breach of lease, or non-waivable legal duties.

text: Parking is provided as a courtesy and may be revoked, reassigned, or charged separately at landlord's discretion.
label: unusual
why: If parking matters to the tenant, this makes it unstable and potentially more expensive.
market_standard: Parking included in rent should be identified and remain available during the term except for reasonable temporary disruptions.
counter: Parking space availability, number, and price should be fixed during the lease term unless tenant agrees otherwise in writing.

text: Amenities including gym, pool, laundry, storage, elevator, package room, and common areas may be closed or removed without rent adjustment.
label: unusual
why: Material amenities can disappear even when they influenced rent.
market_standard: Temporary closures are common, but permanent or extended loss of material amenities should have a remedy.
counter: Temporary amenity closures are allowed for safety, repairs, or legal compliance. Extended or permanent loss of material amenities should allow a reasonable rent adjustment.

text: Tenant agrees to comply with all rules now existing and any future rules landlord adopts, modifies, or revokes at any time.
label: unusual
why: Future rules could materially change the bargain after signing.
market_standard: Rules can change for safety and operations but should be reasonable, prospective, and not materially alter rent or core rights.
counter: Future rules must be reasonable, prospective, uniformly applied, and may not materially increase tenant obligations or reduce lease rights.

text: Any smoke, odor, candle, incense, or vapor condition may result in cleaning, repainting, carpet replacement, and odor remediation fees at landlord's discretion.
label: unusual
why: The trigger is broad and fees are discretionary.
market_standard: No-smoking rules are common, but charges should require actual damage and itemization.
counter: Charges apply only for documented smoke-related damage beyond ordinary wear, supported by itemized invoices.

text: Landlord may approve pets subject to pet rent, pet deposit, pet fees, DNA registration, cleaning charges, and additional rules set at any time.
label: unusual
why: Open-ended pet costs can increase after move-in.
market_standard: Pet fees and rules should be disclosed before signing and remain stable during the term.
counter: All pet deposits, rent, fees, and rules must be listed in the lease and may not increase during the lease term without tenant consent.

text: All animals, including assistance, emotional support, or service animals, require pet rent and nonrefundable pet fees.
label: red-flag
why: Assistance animal charges may violate fair housing rules.
market_standard: Assistance animals should be handled under applicable fair housing accommodation rules, not ordinary pet fee rules.
counter: Assistance animals approved as reasonable accommodations are not subject to pet rent, pet deposits, or pet fees except as allowed by law.
demo_hook: Service animal charged like a pet.

text: The security deposit is nonrefundable and may be applied by landlord in landlord's sole discretion.
label: red-flag
why: Calling a security deposit nonrefundable is often improper and undermines itemization protections.
market_standard: Deposits should be refundable except for lawful, itemized deductions.
counter: Security deposit is refundable except for lawful, itemized deductions for unpaid rent, damage beyond ordinary wear, or other charges allowed by law.

text: Tenant must pay $250 for any lost key, fob, lockout, mailbox key, remote, or access device.
label: unusual
why: A flat fee may exceed actual replacement or lockout costs.
market_standard: Replacement fees should be reasonable and tied to actual costs.
counter: Replacement and lockout fees must be reasonable, disclosed, and limited to actual replacement, vendor, or locksmith costs.

text: All notices to tenant may be sent by email, text, portal, or posting and are deemed received immediately upon sending.
label: unusual
why: Immediate deemed receipt can be unfair if the tenant misses an electronic notice.
market_standard: Notice methods and receipt timing should match applicable law and provide a reasonable delivery period.
counter: Notices are effective only when delivered according to applicable law, with email or portal notices deemed received after a reasonable period unless confirmed earlier.

text: Landlord may relocate tenant to another unit at any time for any reason upon 5 days' notice.
label: red-flag
why: This undermines the tenant's right to the specific unit they rented.
market_standard: Relocation should be limited to emergencies or mutually agreed substitutions.
counter: Tenant may be relocated only for emergency, legal, or habitability reasons, to a comparable or better unit, with reasonable notice and landlord-paid moving costs.
demo_hook: You can be moved with 5 days' notice.

text: Tenant waives claims and rent reduction rights for construction, noise, dust, renovation, interruption, or loss of access caused by landlord or neighboring work.
label: red-flag
why: Extended construction can materially affect living conditions.
market_standard: Temporary disruptions are expected, but substantial interference should preserve legal remedies.
counter: Tenant does not waive remedies for substantial interference with quiet enjoyment, habitability, access, or services caused by landlord-controlled work.

text: Tenant is responsible for all bedbug inspection, treatment, relocation, laundry, disposal, and extermination costs.
label: red-flag
why: Bedbug responsibility often depends on source, notice, and building conditions.
market_standard: Treatment responsibility should follow applicable law and fault, not automatically fall on the tenant.
counter: Bedbug inspection and treatment responsibilities will follow applicable law and documented cause. Tenant is responsible only where tenant caused the infestation.
demo_hook: All bedbug costs pushed to tenant.

text: Appliances are provided for tenant convenience only and landlord has no duty to repair or replace them.
label: unusual
why: If appliances are part of the unit, tenants expect them to work.
market_standard: Included appliances should be maintained unless explicitly excluded before rent is set.
counter: Landlord will maintain and repair appliances provided with the premises unless damage is caused by tenant misuse.
demo_hook: Included appliances are not really included.

text: Landlord is not responsible for interruption of water, heat, gas, electricity, internet, elevator, trash, or other services for any reason.
label: red-flag
why: Essential service interruptions can create habitability issues.
market_standard: Landlord should not disclaim responsibility for essential services within landlord's control.
counter: Landlord remains responsible for essential services and building systems within landlord's control, subject to applicable habitability laws.

text: Tenant will pay a mandatory move-out cleaning fee of $600 regardless of unit condition.
label: unusual
why: Mandatory flat cleaning fees can duplicate deposit deductions.
market_standard: Cleaning charges should reflect actual condition beyond ordinary wear and be disclosed clearly.
counter: Move-out cleaning charges apply only if the unit is not returned in the condition required by the lease, ordinary wear excepted, and must be itemized.
demo_hook: Cleaning fee applies even if spotless.

text: Tenant must promptly notify landlord of needed repairs. Landlord will make repairs required by law within a reasonable time after notice.
label: standard
why: This creates clear repair notice duties for tenant and response duties for landlord.
market_standard: Prompt repair notice and reasonable landlord response are standard.
counter: No change needed.

text: Tenant will pay monthly rent of the stated amount on the first day of each month during the lease term.
label: standard
why: A fixed rent amount and due date are expected in a lease.
market_standard: Rent should be clearly stated and fixed for the term.
counter: No change needed.
